sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Minor Injuries:**
 Minor injuries include superficial wounds, abrasions, minor lacerations, contusions, sprains, and simple fractures. These injuries are not immediately life-threatening but require appropriate assessment and management to prevent complications such as infection, further tissue damage, or functional loss.

 **Epistaxis:**
 Epistaxis, or nosebleed, is bleeding from the nasal cavity, nasopharynx, or nostrils. It is common, affecting up to 60% of people at least once in their lifetime. Most cases are benign and self-limiting, but severe or recurrent episodes may indicate underlying pathology or require advanced intervention

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Minor Injuries:**
 - Localized pain, swelling, or tenderness
- Bruising (contusion)
- Superficial bleeding (abrasion or laceration)
- Limited range of motion (sprain)
- Mild deformity (simple fracture)
- No signs of shock or major trauma

 **Epistaxis:**
 - Bleeding from one or both nostrils
- Blood in the throat or mouth
- Swallowing or spitting blood
- Nasal congestion or blockage
- Dizziness or light-headedness (if blood loss is significant)
- Pallor or signs of shock (rare, in severe cases
- Nausea and Vomiting (frank blood)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Aseptic technique should be used in the treatment of all open wounds
- Swelling, bruising and deformity are classic signs of fractures. Grade 3 sprains can present this way, and you cannot go wrong treating these as fractures until proven otherwise
- Epistaxis can be a result of increased blood pressure with no other signs
- In terms of epistaxis, consider if the patient is on anticoagulant
- If bleeding persists despite anterior measures, suspect posterior bleeding
- Patients with posterior epistaxis may be bleeding more than can be seen
- In a head-to-head study comparing TXA versus anterior packing with lignocaine/epinephrine alone, patients in the TXA group were more than twice as likely to experience cessation in bleeding with 10 minutes
- Do not pack both nostrils simultaneously unless airway is secured

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey

**Wound care:**
 - Control any external haemorrhage with direct pressure
 - Consider CoPhenylcaine the area for analgesia prior to cleaning
 - Ensure wounds cleaned with sterile water, chlorhexidine or other suitable solution
 - Remove visible debris (do not probe too deeply)
 - Cover wounds with suitable dressing (plaster, sterile gauze, etc.)

**Sprains/strains:**
 - Grade I - Mild degree of ankle sprain: Slight tearing of the ligament with mild swelling
 - Grade II - Moderate degree of ankle sprain: incomplete tearing of the ligament with moderate pain and bruising
 - Grade III - Severe degree of ankle sprain: Complete tearing of the ligament with severe swelling, pain (walking is likely impossible), and bruising
  - Consider compression for sprains, strains
  - Elevate injury where practicable
  - Consider ice to area for 2 minutes every 2 hours
  - Advise rest
 - Ensure, where available, consultation with the onsite Health & Injury Prevention Advisor or Physio takes place

**Simple fracture:**
 - Splint simple fractures

**Epistaxis:**
  - Sit patient in appropriate position, discourage swallowing blood to avoid nausea/vomiting
  - Apply firm pressure with finger and thumb, or have patient perform this, to the nares (soft part of nose) for 10min
  - Consider bolster beneath the nose to catch any blood
  - Consider nasal spray containing oxymetazoline if stocked
  - Consider CoPhenylcaine if bleeding continues
  - Consider nasal packing if epistaxis continues

 - Consider OTC pain relief (paracetamol, ibuprofen [not in active bleeding])
 - Consider Methoxyflurane for pain
 - Assess for distal neurovascular compromise where appropriate
 - Vital signs in all patients
 - Consider secondary survey
 - Enquire about tetanus status where relevant

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
**Wound Care:**
  - Consider Steri-strips, wound glue or suturing where appropriate

 **- Epistaxis**
  - If direct pressure has failed, consider nasal packing with sterile dressing
  - Consider applying IV preparation TXA to packing material in consultation with on- call doctor
  - If bleeding persists despite anterior measures, suspect posterior bleeding
 - Consider antibiotics only in consultation with the on-call doctor
 - Consider analgesia

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Lithium dust, commonly generated during mining, can cause irritation and inflammation of the nasal passages, potentially leading to epistaxis
- Epistaxis Patient education on prevention: humidification, gentle nose care, avoid trauma
